elements.sent-2411-869: Consider the map \begin{equation}R= H\circ h_1^{-1} \circ f \circ h_1\circ H^{-1} in\,\, H(\D) H\circ h_2^{-1}\circ g \circ h_2\circ H^{-1} in\,\, H(\widehat \C\setminus \D). \end{equation} Note that the two definitions agree on $J=H(\mathbb S^1)$ because $h$ conjugates $f$ to $g$ and $h_2= h\circ h_1$ on $\mathbb S^1$.

elements.sent-2411-872: Similarly, $h_2\circ H^{-1}$ is a conformal map from $H(\widehat\C\setminus \bar \D)$ onto $\widehat \C\setminus \overline \D$, and we let $\psi$ be its inverse.
elements.sent-2411-873: By Carath\'eodory's theorem, $\phi$ and $\psi$ extend homeomorphically to the closures of their domains and conjugate $f$ and $g$ to $R$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-876: It remains to show that $R$ is holomorphic on $H(\mathbb S^1)$, which will imply that $R$ is rational.
elements.sent-2411-877: Note that $R$ is a branched cover with no critical points on $H(\mathbb S^1)$.
elements.sent-2411-878: Thus, each point of $H(\mathbb S^1)$ has a neighborhood in which $R$ is conformal.
elements.sent-2411-879: By Theorem \ref{theorem:removable , $H(\mathbb S^1)$ is locally conformally removable, so $R$ is holomorphic on $H(\mathbb S^1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-880: Finally, we show the ultimate uniqueness statement in Theorem \ref{theorem:intro_mating .}
elements.sent-2411-881: Suppose that there exist another Jordan curve $J_0$ with complementary regions $A_0,B_0\subset \widehat\C\setminus J_0$, a rational map $R_0$, and conformal maps $\phi_0\colon \overline \D\to \overline A_0$, $\psi\colon \overline \D\to \overline B_0$ such that $\phi_0$ conjugates $f$ to $ R_0$, $\psi_0$ conjugates $g$ to $R_0$, and $\phi=\psi\circ h$ on $\mathbb S^1$.
elements.sent-2411-882: We define \begin{equation}G(z)=  \phi_0\circ \phi^{-1}(z), z\in \overline A \psi_0\circ \psi^{-1}(z), z\in \overline B \end{equation} and note that the two definitions agree on $J=\partial A=\partial B$.
elements.sent-2411-883: The map $G$ is a homeomorphism of $\widehat \C$ that is conformal in $\widehat \C\setminus J$ and conjugates $R$ to $R_0$.
elements.sent-2411-884: By Theorem \ref{theorem:removable , $J$ is conformally removable, so $G$ is conformal in $\widehat \C$, and thus it is a M\"obius transformation.}
